exercises.title: Global Scope Basics
exercises.description: Variables defined at the top level (module level) are global. Print variables from different parts of the program to show they are accessible throughout.
exercises.starterCode:
# Global variables — accessible everywhere in this file
app_name = "Python Learner"
version = "1.0"
MAX_SCORE = 100

# These can be read anywhere
print(app_name)
print(version)
print(MAX_SCORE)

# They can be used in expressions
print(f"{app_name} v{version} (max score: {MAX_SCORE})")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Python Learner
1.0
100
Python Learner v1.0 (max score: 100)
hints: Variables at the top level (not inside a function or block) are global.
Global variables can be read anywhere in the same file.

exercises.title: Local Scope in Functions
exercises.description: Variables created inside a function exist only during that function call (local scope). They cannot be accessed from outside.
exercises.starterCode:
greeting = "Hello from global scope"

def show_local():
    # This variable is LOCAL to this function
    local_message = "Hello from local scope"
    print(local_message)
    print(greeting)   # Can READ global from inside a function

show_local()

# TODO: Print the global greeting (accessible here)
print(greeting)

# Trying to print local_message here would raise NameError
# print(local_message)   # This line would crash — local_message doesn't exist here
print("local_message is not accessible outside the function")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Hello from local scope
Hello from global scope
Hello from global scope
local_message is not accessible outside the function
hints: Local variables only exist while the function is running.
A function can READ global variables, but cannot modify them without 'global'.

exercises.title: Shadowing and Name Lookup
exercises.description: When a local variable has the same name as a global, the local one 'shadows' the global inside the function. See how name lookup works.
exercises.starterCode:
x = "global x"

def demonstrate_shadowing():
    x = "local x"   # This shadows the global x inside this function
    print(x)         # Prints local x

def read_global():
    # No local x here — reads the global
    print(x)

demonstrate_shadowing()   # Should print 'local x'
read_global()             # Should print 'global x'
print(x)                  # Should print 'global x' — global was NOT changed
exercises.expectedOutput:
local x
global x
global x
hints: Assigning inside a function creates a local variable — it does NOT change the global.
After demonstrate_shadowing() returns, the local x is gone.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Scope Means Where a Variable Lives
warmup.description: At this stage, all your variables are global. Functions introduce local scope.
warmup.starterCode:
# At the module level, variables are global
course = "Python Fundamentals"
lesson = 9

print(f"Course: {course}")
print(f"Lesson: {lesson}")
print(f"Both are accessible anywhere in this file.")
warmup.expectedOutput:
Course: Python Fundamentals
Lesson: 9
Both are accessible anywhere in this file.
